edges.description: Interface inflammation and keratinocyte injury produce papules, crusting, and ulceration.
Epidermal necrosis can heal with hypopigmented macules or varioliform scars.
External antigenic stimuli may trigger cutaneous immune activation in susceptible individuals.

nodes.description: Macrolide or tetracycline antibiotics are commonly used in practice, particularly in pediatric PL, though response is variable.

evidence.reference_title: Pityriasis lichenoides: assessment of 41 pediatric patients.
evidence.snippet: There was remission in 71.9% (n = 23), with 56.6% (n = 17) of those who used antibiotic therapy and 80% (n = 4) of those who had phototherapy.

nodes.description: Lichenoid/interface inflammation injures epidermal keratinocytes, leading to crusting, necrosis, ulceration, and post-inflammatory pigmentary change.

evidence.reference_title: Acute lichenoid and varioliform pityriasis in a pediatric patient.
evidence.snippet: multiple erythematous lesions that disappeared leaving hypopigmented macules.

nodes.description: Methotrexate has been reported among systemic treatment modalities for pityriasis lichenoides when disease is refractory or more severe.

evidence.reference_title: Systematic review of the efficacies and adverse effects of treatments for pityriasis lichenoides.
evidence.snippet: Treatment modalities included in these articles were phototherapy, antibiotics, methotrexate, pyrimethamine and trisulfapyrimidine, corticosteroids and conservative treatment.

nodes.description: Crops of erythematous papules are a core manifestation.

evidence.reference_title: Acute lichenoid and varioliform pityriasis in a pediatric patient.
evidence.snippet: characterized by multiple small or large erythematous plaques spread over the trunk and extremities.

nodes.description: Phototherapy, especially UVB-based regimens, has the strongest remission signal among reported treatment modalities but evidence remains mostly uncontrolled.

evidence.reference_title: Systematic review of the efficacies and adverse effects of treatments for pityriasis lichenoides.
evidence.snippet: Of these treatments, phototherapy led to complete remission in the highest proportion of patients

evidence.reference_title: Phototherapy for Pityriasis Lichenoides in the Pediatric Population: A Review of the Published Literature.
evidence.snippet: Phototherapy shows promising results and a favorable side-effect profile in the treatment of PL.

evidence.reference_title: Pityriasis lichenoides in an Asian population.
evidence.snippet: Itch was present in the majority.

nodes.description: PLEVA lesions reflect cutaneous immune activation with lichenoid/interface inflammation and keratinocyte injury, producing papulonecrotic eruptions.

evidence.reference_title: Acute lichenoid and varioliform pityriasis in a pediatric patient.
evidence.snippet: lymphocytic vasculitis (LV) with focal epidermal necrosis consistent with acute pityriasis lichenoides (PL) was identified.

nodes.description: Topical corticosteroids are often trialed for symptomatic inflammatory skin lesions, but systematic-review evidence does not establish a definitive evidence-based regimen.

evidence.reference_title: Systematic review of the efficacies and adverse effects of treatments for pityriasis lichenoides.
evidence.snippet: topical corticosteroids were found to have been trialled in the highest number of patients.

nodes.description: Infectious outbreaks, SARS-CoV-2 infection or vaccination, and other exposures have been temporally associated with pityriasis lichenoides, but causal proof is limited and many cases are idiopathic.

evidence.reference_title: Pityriasis lichenoides: assessment of 41 pediatric patients.
evidence.snippet: The frequency peaks coincided with infectious outbreaks.
This review cannot determine causality. However, a temporal association was observed with the case reports
